ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Orh Luak or its vegan variant. 'Omelette food product' is the closest defensible parent class covering egg-and-starch pan-fried dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Omelette) is the closest parent. Vegan Orh Luak uses tapioca starch batter with tofu instead of oysters and eggs; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for this dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Orh Luak or its vegan variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Vegan Orh Luak as served in a Singapore hawker-style setting, approximately 300 g, comprising tofu, straw mushrooms, starch batter, bean sprouts, and chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster omelette (Or Luak) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central component estimation for vegan adaptation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for traditional Orh Luak (oyster omelette). The vegan adaptation replaces oysters and egg with firm tofu and straw mushrooms. Macros are adapted from HPB Foodo's Or Luak entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for firm tofu (NDB 16126) and straw mushrooms. Energy and macros are adjusted downward for the absence of oysters and egg yolk fat, and upward for tofu protein. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed starch-based Asian dishes — analogous estimate based on tapioca starch omelette and tofu composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegan Orh Luak. GI is estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tapioca-starch-based dishes (GI ~55) and the moderating effect of firm tofu protein and fat content, which lower the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 43.5) / 100 ≈ 22.6, rounded to 22.5. Serving carbohydrate = 14.5 g/100g × 300 g = 43.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-sodium chilli sauce to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 52 and a glycemic load of approximately 22.5 per standard serving; tapioca starch batter contributes rapidly digestible carbohydrates.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion of approximately 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the moderate glycemic load from the starch batter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Vegan formulation eliminates dietary cholesterol; saturated fat is low at 1.1 g per 100 g; tofu provides isoflavones with potential cardioprotective effects.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The vegan adaptation is relatively heart-friendly compared to the traditional oyster omelette; however, sodium content remains a concern and portion control is advised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegan Orh Luak is a plant-based adaptation of the classic Singaporean hawker dish Orh Luak (oyster omelette), traditionally made with fresh oysters, egg, and tapioca starch batter pan-fried in lard. In this vegan version, oysters and egg are replaced with fried firm tofu and straw mushrooms, while the batter retains its characteristic tapioca and sweet potato starch base, yielding a crispy-edged, chewy-centred pancake. The dish is served with a house-made vegan chilli sauce and typically garnished with bean sprouts and spring onion. Orh Luak originates from Fujian Chinese culinary tradition and is deeply embedded in Singapore and Penang hawker culture. The vegan variant preserves the textural contrast — crispy exterior, soft starchy interior — while delivering plant-based protein from tofu (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g) and eliminating dietary cholesterol. The dish has a moderate glycemic index of approximately 52 due to the tapioca starch batter, and a glycemic load of approximately 22.5 per standard 300 g serving. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and chilli sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Clinically, the vegan formulation offers a lower saturated fat and cholesterol profile compared to the traditional version, making it a more cardiovascular-friendly option, though sodium management remains relevant. The dish is suitable for vegans, vegetarians, and dairy-free diets, but contains soy allergens and may contain gluten from soy sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for Vegan Orh Luak exists in HPB Foodo or any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo's traditional Orh Luak entry with component-level adjustments for the vegan substitution (tofu replacing oysters and egg). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish would be required for high-confidence classification. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a fully vegan dish containing no animal-derived ingredients that contribute dietary cholesterol. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as no animal products or B12-fortified ingredients are present in the standard formulation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based dish; no UV-exposed mushrooms or fortified ingredients are specified. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no partially hydrogenated oils are used. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from tofu and vegetable oil composition). 7. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the adapted source basis; specifically: no direct measurement data available from HPB Foodo or USDA for these micronutrients in this composite dish configuration. 8. GI set to 52 as an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for tapioca-starch-based Asian dishes, moderated by tofu protein and fat content. 9. Cuisine reclassified from input 'Global' to 'Singaporean' based on dish origin and multilingual naming requirements. 10. Food group retained as 'Grains and staples' consistent with the starch-dominant batter base of the dish.
